title: Dog Biting and Scratching Tail Base: Causes and Relief
meta_desc: Find out why your dog is biting the base of its tail, the urgency level for vet care, and how photos help with a quick veterinary diagnosis.
content_html:
What is causing your dog to scratch and bite its tail base?

When a dog focuses intense scratching, biting, or chewing at the base of the tail, it is most often a sign of Flea Allergy Dermatitis (FAD). Even if you do not see active fleas, a single bite can trigger an intense allergic reaction in sensitive dogs. Other common causes include impacted anal glands, environmental allergies, or skin infections.

Is it an emergency?

Urgency level: Medium. While this behavior is rarely a life-threatening emergency, it causes significant discomfort and can quickly lead to 'hot spots' or secondary bacterial infections. You should seek veterinary care within 24 to 48 hours, especially if the skin appears raw, bleeding, or is oozing fluid.

Why a photo helps triage

Providing a clear, close-up photo of the skin at the base of the tail is incredibly helpful for a triage professional. A visual assessment allows us to look for flea dirt, see the extent of the inflammation, and determine if the area is infected. This helps us prioritize your pet's needs and provide more accurate immediate advice to keep them comfortable.

faqs.question: Could it be fleas even if I don't see any?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dogs with flea allergies react to the flea saliva. The dog may groom the flea off, but the allergic reaction persists for days or weeks afterward.

faqs.question: What can I do at home to help?
faqs.answer: The most important step is to prevent further self-trauma. Using an Elizabethan collar (cone) can stop your dog from biting the area until you can reach a veterinarian.

faqs.question: How do I know if it is an anal gland issue?
faqs.answer: If your dog is 'scooting' their rear on the floor in addition to biting the tail base, it may indicate that their anal glands are full or impacted.
